title: Playbook - Azure Logic Apps From External APIs To SharePoint Storage
applies_to: Logic Apps workflows that retrieve data from external APIs and store results in SharePoint lists or document libraries
prerequisites: Decision on trigger model: Request for programmatic invocation or Recurrence for scheduled execution
Authentication plan for the source API
Authentication plan for SharePoint target access
Target SharePoint site, list, or document library resolution strategy
required_permissions: Permissions for the source API according to that API's auth model
SharePoint connector or Graph permissions sufficient for the target list or document library
Managed identity or connection permissions for every Azure resource or connector involved
request_sequence: Start the workflow with either a Request trigger for programmatic calls or a Recurrence trigger for scheduled polling.
Call the source API with the built-in HTTP action or another appropriate connector.
Parse and normalize the response by using Parse JSON plus data operations such as Select or Filter array.
Decide how to store the result in SharePoint: list items for tabular records, or files in a document library for documents, raw payloads, CSV, or transformed files.
If writing structured rows, use SharePoint list operations through the SharePoint connector or Microsoft Graph list APIs.
If writing files, use SharePoint file operations through the SharePoint connector, or use Microsoft Graph driveItem APIs when the workflow already uses Graph or needs finer file semantics.
Handle paging, duplicate detection, and update-vs-create rules explicitly so repeated runs do not create uncontrolled duplicates.

confirmed_facts.fact: The SharePoint connector supports actions such as Create file, Update file, Delete file, Get files (properties only), Create item, Update item, Delete item, and Get items.
Logic Apps built-in HTTP operations can call external APIs, and Parse JSON can turn a JSON payload into typed tokens for downstream actions.
The Request trigger creates a callable endpoint for a workflow, while the Recurrence trigger is the built-in scheduling trigger for time-based execution.
For Logic Apps programmatic API patterns, managed identity is the preferred authentication model whenever the target connector or destination supports Microsoft Entra authentication.

implementation_notes: Use SharePoint lists when the API response maps naturally to rows and columns that need update or delete semantics. Use SharePoint files when the result is a document, attachment, exported CSV, raw JSON snapshot, or large payload artifact.

If the workflow already relies on Microsoft Graph for SharePoint site, list, or drive resolution, keep the write path in Graph rather than mixing two models without a reason. Otherwise, the SharePoint connector can be the simpler designer-first option.
For idempotent loads, persist a source-system key in a SharePoint column so later runs can find and update an existing item instead of always creating a new one.
For list writes, normalize the payload before entering any foreach loop. For file writes, decide on naming and overwrite behavior up front.

If the source API needs app-only auth and the SharePoint target can also be reached with Entra-backed auth, Logic Apps with managed identity is usually a better fit than delegated connector platforms.
common_failures: Using a list for payloads that really belong in a file or document library, leading to awkward schema or size problems.
Ignoring source API pagination and only persisting the first page.
Recreating duplicate SharePoint items because the workflow has no source-key or upsert strategy.
Using a connector auth mode that cannot satisfy the required service-to-service pattern.
Writing directly to SharePoint without first shaping nested API data, resulting in unusable list rows.
